attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a 12-hour history of increasing pain in the back of her right eye, associated with nausea and right-sided headache. She reports that her vision from that eye has become blurred. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears unwell with vital signs within normal limits. Her pinhole visual acuity is 6/60 in the right eye and 6/9 in the left eye. Slit lamp examination of the right eye demonstrates a hazy, edematous cornea with severely injected conjunctival vessels. The pupil is mid-dilated and non-responsive to light.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is acute angle-closure glaucoma, with the stem describing a classical presentation. This condition involves a sudden onset increase in intraocular pressure due to obstruction of the aqueous drainage route in the eye. It typically occurs when the peripheral iris bows forward to the lens, causing a "pupillary block".

The classical clinical presentation of acute angle-closure glaucoma, as seen in this patient, includes a red, painful eye with associated nausea, brow headache, blurred vision, and haloes around lights. These symptoms occur due to increased intraocular pressure and associated corneal edema. The examination demonstrates markedly increased intraocular pressure, a fixed mid-dilated pupil (due to pupillary block), and an associated hazy cornea. Risk factors include age over 60, reduced anterior chamber depth (regardless of hyperopia or myopia), and female sex (with a 4:1 female to male ratio).

Acute conjunctivitis typically presents with redness, discharge, and irritation but does not cause severe pain, visual loss, or pupillary changes as seen in this case.

Microbial keratitis can cause eye pain and reduced vision, but it usually presents with a corneal infiltrate or ulcer, which is not mentioned in this case. It also wouldn't explain the mid-dilated, non-reactive pupil.

Acute orbital compartment syndrome is a severe condition that can cause proptosis, restricted eye movements, and vision loss, but it doesn't typically cause the specific pupillary changes or corneal edema described here.

Vitreous hemorrhage can cause sudden vision loss but doesn't typically cause the severe pain, nausea, or anterior segment changes (corneal edema, pupillary changes) seen in this patient.

Immediate treatment for acute angle-closure glaucoma is required, typically with intravenous or oral acetazolamide. Topical anti-glaucoma agents are also used. Definitive treatment in primary angle-closure glaucoma includes laser iridotomy. Prompt recognition and management are crucial to prevent permanent vision loss.
learningPoints: Acute angle closure glaucoma is an emergency, requiring rapid ocular decompression.
Classic symptoms of acute angle closure glaucoma include eye pain, headache, nausea, and blurred vision.
Examination findings in acute angle closure glaucoma include reduced visual acuity, corneal edema, conjunctival injection, and a mid-dilated, non-reactive pupil.
references: Ehlers JP, Shah CP. The Wills eye manual: Office and emergency room diagnosis and treatment of eye disease. Philadelphia: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2016
Murray D. Emergency management: angle-closure glaucoma. Community Eye Health. 2018;31(103):64. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6253313/

Liang Y, Shen R, Zhou W, Fan S, Chan PP, Tham CCY, et al. Prevalence and Ocular Biometric Characteristics of Myopia in Primary Angle Closure Disease in Rural China: The Handan Eye Study. Invest Ophthalmol Vis Sci. 2022 Nov 1;63(12):19. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36374513/

van Romunde, Saskia H. M., Thepass, Gijs, Lemij, Hans G., Is Hyperopia an Important Risk Factor for PACG in the Dutch Population?—A Case Control Study, Journal of Ophthalmology. 2013:630481. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1155/2013/630481
